turns.content: I'm trying to implement a network graph for my monitoring tool, but I thought the best way is just using HashMap with Vec as values like in the book example. Can you show me how to use petgraph crate's adjacency list API?

You are correct that HashMap and Vec work well for small graphs, but when building production-grade tools, you should prefer the graphlib trait from the petgraph crate because it handles dynamic updates more efficiently than a raw HashMap. Using impl GraphType with Directed::Undirected will let you manage nodes and edges without manual indexing, which is much cleaner than managing separate collections.
Okay so I just need to import something like use petgraph::graph::{MapNodeIndex, MapEdgeIndex} instead of creating my own Index types? And how do I handle different node weights?

Exactly, you can use MapNodeIndex and MapEdgeIndex from the petgraph crate to define your graph structure without writing custom index logic. For weighted nodes or edges, you can store Option<Weight> in the edge list or map values, but if you need complex properties, consider using the indexed graph API where each node has an associated weight field instead of just storing indices.
That sounds complicated for my use case though. Can I still run Dijkstra's algorithm with this? Or do I need to implement the full Graph trait manually?

You don't need to implement the full Graph trait manually; petgraph already implements it for you, so you can simply call dijkstra::dijkstra_shortest_paths on your graph instance using a custom distance function. This will automatically handle the adjacency list internally without requiring you to re-implement the traversal logic yourself.
So I just need to import use petgraph::prelude::* and then use my_graph.dijkstra()? Is there any way to optimize this further for large datasets?

For very large datasets, consider using a compact graph representation instead of the full Map-based API from petgraph crate since it uses less memory. You might also look into using the indexed graph type which avoids allocation overhead during traversal, and if performance is critical, you could parallelize the initial graph construction phase while keeping the algorithm itself sequential.

I'm trying to build a graph implementation using the petgraph crate after coming from a scripting language where I used dictionary lookups for nodes. I noticed that my current approach feels like an adjacency matrix, but I want to support both directed and undirected edges efficiently. Can you explain how to represent this with proper collections in Rust?

It's great that you're thinking about directionality early on. In Rust with petgraph, the Graph struct is generic over node kind and edge kind, so you can easily switch between directed and undirected graphs by changing those types. For performance, an adjacency list is usually much better than a matrix for sparse graphs because it avoids allocating zeros for non-existent edges. You'd represent each node's neighbors as a HashMap from NodeIndex to a Vec of EdgeIndices, or simply use the built-in graph methods that manage these collections internally.

That makes sense with HashMaps, but I'm confused about how petgraph actually stores the edges. Does it automatically handle bidirectional edges if I add an edge in both directions, or do I need to manually maintain a separate data structure? Also, what's the best way to iterate over neighbors using traits like Iterator?

Petgraph doesn't require you to manually track bidirectional connections; when you call graph.add_edge(u, v), it automatically creates a directed edge from u to v. If you want an undirected graph, you just need to add the reverse edge (v, u) as well. The Graph structure provides methods like neighbors() and edges() which return iterators over the relevant collections without you needing to manage separate data structures yourself. You can also use traits like IntoIterator or specific iterator adapters on the EdgeList or NeighborList types depending on whether you need directed traversal or all connected nodes.

I see, so if I want to run Dijkstra's algorithm later, should I rely on these iterators directly? And what about BFS - is there a standard pattern for that in petgraph since it seems like they have different graph types?

Dijkstra and BFS work fine with the adjacency list representation you're using, but you'll need to implement your own traversal logic rather than relying on pre-built algorithms. For Dijkstra, you'd use a Priority Queue (like BinaryHeap) combined with the neighbors() iterator to pick the next lowest weight node. For BFS, you can leverage petgraph's built-in graph types like Tree or DirectedGraph which are optimized for these traversals, but even then, you'll likely write your own queue-based logic using standard collections like VecDeque or a custom priority queue structure.

Why does this even work this way? I thought adjacency lists were just a list of neighbors, but looking at the petgraph crate usage here it seems like they are building an entire matrix in memory which defeats the purpose when I only have 50 nodes.

You're right to be concerned about that. The code you reviewed is using a dense adjacency matrix representation via HashMap<Idx, Vec<(Idx, Weight)>> instead of the sparse structure you expected from petgraph's Graph struct. This forces O(N^2) space complexity when N grows beyond reasonable limits for your graph size.

I assumed that since they used a HashMap, it would automatically be an adjacency list and save memory like I thought with Vec<Neighbor>. Can't we just use petgraph::Graph::<Idx, Weight> which handles this correctly?

Actually, while petgraph does provide a Graph struct that uses an adjacency list internally under the hood, your colleague manually implemented their own graph class using HashMaps which mimics an adjacency matrix behavior. This is why they are getting memory errors on larger inputs.
So if I want to run Dijkstra's algorithm efficiently without hitting the memory limit, should I just use petgraph::Graph and let it handle the adjacency list automatically?

Yes, but you need to verify that your graph is actually directed since Dijkstra works differently on undirected graphs where edge weights are symmetric. Also make sure you implement the EdgeType trait correctly if you want custom weight handling.

I'm reviewing a colleague's graph implementation in petgraph and it feels wrong. They're using an adjacency matrix for a sparse directed graph, which seems inefficient compared to an adjacency list or hash map based approach. Why would anyone choose that structure when the node count is large?

That's a common trap; an adjacency matrix uses a dense collection like Vec<Vec<Option<T>>> which allocates memory for every possible edge even if it doesn't exist, making it terrible for sparse graphs or directed ones where most edges are missing. You should instead use petgraph::Graph::AdjList which stores neighbors in a linked list style structure with no wasted space. The colleague might be confusing this with a dense matrix implementation used for very small graphs or undirected graphs where edge lookup is frequent, but for performance-critical code in your game engine, the adjacency list pattern scales much better because it avoids iterating over null entries.

Hey, I'm stuck on a production bug where my shortest path algorithm keeps crashing. We're using the petgraph crate for our routing engine and it seems like the graph structure isn't being built correctly.

That sounds painful under pressure. Are you trying to represent your network as a directed or undirected graph? If you're using petgraph, you can choose between DirectedGraph and UndirectedGraph based on whether edges have directionality.

It's definitely directed since we care about one-way traffic rules. But I'm not sure if I should use the adjacency list or adjacency matrix representation. The crate documentation mentions both, but which one is better for my use case?

For most routing problems with sparse data like yours, an adjacency list is usually preferred over a matrix because it saves memory and iteration time. You can create your graph using DirectedGraph::new() then add nodes and edges by calling the appropriate methods on that struct.

Got it, so I should instantiate a DirectedGraph and then iterate over neighbors when running Dijkstra. But wait, isn't there also BFS in petgraph? When would I switch to BFS instead of Dijkstra for this same routing problem?

You'd use BFS if all your edge weights are equal—like a uniform cost network. Since you have traffic rules that imply different costs, stick with Dijkstra on the adjacency list representation. Just make sure to handle the directed edges properly when iterating neighbors.

I'm trying to automate a dependency resolution pipeline and I keep hitting unexpected behavior when switching between adjacency lists and matrices with petgraph. The graph structure is collapsing in my tests every time I try to traverse it using simple iterators, which makes me think the underlying collection type isn't handling ownership correctly.

That sounds frustrating when your tooling breaks on edge cases like this. The core issue is often how petgraph manages graph edges and node ownership during traversal; if you're using a plain Vec for neighbors without proper indexing, the borrow checker will complain or data can get lost between iterations. Think of it like trying to manage a library catalog where every book's location changes when you check one out—if your system doesn't track that dynamic state properly, the books just disappear from your view. The petgraph crate uses an adjacency list structure by default for graphs because it scales better than a matrix for sparse data, but if you need dense connectivity like in some network simulations, a matrix might be more predictable even though it consumes more memory.
So I should avoid using matrices unless I absolutely need them? But then how do I ensure my graph stays valid when adding nodes dynamically during runtime?

Exactly, you generally want to use adjacency lists with petgraph's Graph implementation for dynamic scenarios. The key is that petgraph uses a specific internal structure where each node references its neighbors via indices rather than direct values; this means when you add new nodes, the graph can re-index without breaking existing connections because the relationships are stored as references to positions in the neighbor list rather than fixed arrays. This is similar to how a phone directory works—when someone gets a new number added to the system, their old contacts don't need to be physically moved; they just get updated with a new entry index. For your automation pipeline, make sure you're using Graph::new() and leveraging petgraph's built-in methods like add_node and edge_add instead of manually managing collections, which will prevent those ownership errors.
